pattern_summary: The dominant AI pattern in trading journals as of 2026: after a single trade closes, an LLM is given the trade's structured fields (entry/exit/stop/MFE/MAE/duration/setup-tag/notes/screenshots) and produces a narrative post-mortem — what went right, what went wrong, what to improve. Output is typically a chat-card, a paragraph of prose, or a structured 'lessons' list. It is the EASIEST AI feature to ship (closed-trade data is already structured) but also the SHALLOWEST coaching value (n=1, no statistical signal, mostly platitudes).

ai_features.feature_name: Common names: 'AI Trade Review' (generic), 'Cypher AI' (TraderSync), 'Zella Insights' (Tradezella, scoped to behaviors), 'AI Coach' (TradeJournal.AI / MRT), 'Trade Autopsy' (community term).
ai_features.ai_scope: Per-trade — a single closed trade is the analysis unit.
ai_features.ai_proactive_vs_reactive: Mostly REACTIVE (button: 'Review with AI'). Some (TraderSync Elite) push insights post-import. PROACTIVE per-trade is rare because it gets noisy fast at >5 trades/day.
ai_features.ai_minimum_trade_threshold: Per-trade itself has no threshold (always n=1), but its USEFULNESS requires reference-data (the same setup's history, the user's prior trades). Without that context the AI produces generic advice.
ai_features.ai_underlying_tech: Mostly OpenAI GPT-4-class via vendor-hosted API. Tradezella details unclear. MRT Journal explicitly uses Claude. Some use classical-ML (Edgewonk's Edge Finder) layered with light NLG.
ai_features.output_format: Text paragraph, structured-card with sections (What worked / What didn't / Next time), or chat-thread allowing follow-up questions.
ai_features.notable_prompts: Examples reverse-engineered from output: (1) 'Analyse this trade. Was the entry timing aligned with the user's stated setup? What was the MFE-vs-realised gap? Suggest one improvement.' (2) Cypher-style: 'Compare this trade to the user's last 10 same-setup trades. Where does it fall in the distribution? Any rule-violation tags?' (3) Zella-style (narrower, behavior-focused): 'Detect any of {revenge-trade, overtrade, FOMO-entry, premature-exit, no-stop, oversized}.'

playbook_depth.setup_level_ai: Weak in most implementations — per-trade autopsy rarely connects upward to setup-level aggregation. Cypher AI and Zella Insights start to bridge this (e.g., 'most rule-violations happen in this setup, in afternoon sessions'), but the analysis unit remains the single trade.
playbook_depth.rule_adherence_scoring: Some implementations (TraderSync, Tradezella) flag 'non-adherent' but the rule-set is often shallow (max-risk-per-trade, max-trades-per-day) rather than per-setup playbook rules.

coaching_depth.coaching_depth_level: Mostly surface_metrics + light pattern-call-outs. Genuine deep_pattern_analysis (causal hypothesis + actionable next-step) is rare because n=1 prevents statistical claims, and the AI has to either be vague ('consider tighter stop') or invent context.
coaching_depth.critique: Three persistent weaknesses: (1) GENERIC OUTPUT — without history-context the LLM produces textbook trading-advice indistinguishable from a YouTube comment. (2) NO CAUSAL CLAIMS POSSIBLE at n=1 — 'this stop was too tight' could be variance, not error; the AI bluffs. (3) ALERT FATIGUE — users get a paragraph per trade; in a day-trading session that's 15+ paragraphs, no one reads them. Most active users disable per-trade autopsy after week 2.

concrete_implementations: TraderSync Cypher AI ($79.95/mo Elite): per-trade insights + chat-followup, requires 100+ trades for context. Strongest implementation.
Tradezella Zella Insights: behavior-scoped (revenge/scale-out/overtrading) rather than free-form — narrower but more actionable.
TradeJournal.AI: per-trade autopsy + 'AI Coach reads weekly reviews' — combines per-trade with periodic.
MRT Journal: Claude-API-powered per-trade analysis with explicit own-API-key model.
Edgewonk Edge Finder: classical-ML pattern detection emailed weekly — adjacent pattern, not LLM-based but solves the same coaching gap.
opportunity_gap_crypto_perp: For crypto-perps the per-trade autopsy can be uniquely deep because the structured-data is richer: each trade carries multiple TPs (partial-close legs), funding-rate-during-hold, liquidation-cluster-distance, OI-delta. A Tradezella-equivalent doesn't even MODEL partial-closes correctly (treats them as separate trades). Opportunity: per-trade autopsy that natively understands 'TP1 was hit but TP2/TP3 weren't — was that because the move ran out of steam (look at OI/funding-flip) or because price action looked weak (look at tape)?' No competitor does this in 2026.
uncertain: Exact internal prompts used by Cypher AI / Zella Insights / MRT — reverse-engineered from output, not from vendor documentation
User-retention numbers for per-trade autopsy features (no public data)
